metadata.description: Luganda verb tenses: structure, naming, and full conjugation examples
metadata.verification_notes: Corrections: (1) t_001 sleep everyday form corrected from 'yeebaka' to 'yeebase'. (2) t_003 sleep past form corrected from 'yeebaka' to 'yawebaka'. (3) t_004 walk future corrected from 'ali tambula' (space) to 'alitambula'. (4) t_004 run future corrected from 'ajja kuduka' to 'ajja kudduka' (double d). (5) t_004 stand future corrected from 'aliyimiririra' (extra ri) to 'aliyimirira'. (6) t_005 Ekyali Kyaggwa: the example 'afumba = cooked (completed)' was WRONG — afumba is present tense. Replaced with correct completed past examples. (7) subject prefix plural 'you' flag cleared — Mugenda is correct standard Luganda.

introduction: Luganda verbs change their form to show when an action happens. There are four main tenses used in everyday Luganda, plus a completed past form. Learning these tenses is essential for speaking and writing correctly.

tenses.description: Used for actions that happen regularly, every day, or habitually. Like saying 'I eat every day' in English — not happening right now at this exact moment, but it always happens as a pattern.
tenses.time_marker: buli lunaku (every day), bulijjo (always), buli kiseera (every time)
tenses.verb_pattern: Subject prefix + verb stem. For third person (he/she): A- prefix + verb stem.

examples.note: CORRECTED: 'yeebaka' was wrong here. Yeebase = habitual/present state of sleeping.

sentence_examples.english: I go to work every day.
My son goes to school every day.
He/she drinks a lot of water (habitually).

tenses.description: Used for actions happening at this very moment. Like 'She is eating right now' — the action is actively in progress. The time word 'kakati' (now) often signals this tense.
tenses.verb_pattern: Same prefix structure as everyday tense. Context and time words signal present vs habitual. Some verbs use a stative form for present state.

sentence_examples.english: Their mother is eating food (right now).
It is late, she is eating while standing.
Now she is looking out of the window.

tenses.description: Used for actions that already happened and are completed. In Luganda, the past tense for third person (he/she) typically changes the verb prefix to 'ya-'. For first person (I), the prefix changes to 'na-'.
tenses.time_marker: jjo (yesterday), luli (last time), edda (in the past), awo (then/at that time)
tenses.verb_pattern: ya + verb stem for he/she. na + verb stem for I. ba + verb stem for they.

examples.note: CORRECTED: previous form 'yeebaka' was the infinitive form, not past. Correct past = yawebaka.

sentence_examples.luganda: Omusajja yafumba ate omukyala n'asiika enva.
sentence_examples.english: The man cooked while the woman fried the sauce.
The woman washed the clothes while the man dried them.
I went to the market yesterday.

tenses.description: Used for actions that have not yet happened but will happen. Luganda future tense is formed either by using 'ajja + infinitive' (will do) or by the prefix 'ali-' before the verb stem for third person.
tenses.time_marker: enkya (tomorrow), mu maaso (in the future), leero emmande (later today)
tenses.verb_pattern: ajja + full infinitive (e.g., ajja okulya) OR ali + verb stem (e.g., alirya). Both are correct. Future forms are written as one word — no spaces.

examples.note: CORRECTED: previous form 'ali tambula' had a space. Written as one word: alitambula.
CORRECTED: previous form 'ajja kuduka' was missing the double d. Correct infinitive is okudduka.
CORRECTED: previous form 'aliyimiririra' had an extra 'ri'. Correct form: aliyimirira.

tenses.description: Used for actions that were completed in the past and whose result is still relevant or felt now. Similar to 'has cooked' or 'has gone' in English. This tense is formed differently from the simple past (ekyayita). It typically uses a different verb ending.
tenses.verb_pattern: The completed past often uses the ending '-iye' or '-ye' added to the verb stem. This is more complex than simple past and needs careful study.

examples.completed_past_meaning: he/she has cooked (the food is ready now)
examples.note: The -ye ending signals completed action with a present result.

examples.completed_past_meaning: he/she has gone (and is currently away)
examples.note: Context distinguishes simple past from completed state.

examples.completed_past_meaning: he/she has arrived (and is here now)
examples.note: Azze is the completed past of okujja — often heard in greetings.

tenses.important_note: The completed past tense is still being documented from source material. The examples above are reliable. A full conjugation table for this tense needs expert verification before teaching.

sentence_examples.english: He/she has arrived / He/she is here.
He/she has cooked the food (it is ready).

tense_summary_table.description: Quick reference table showing all tenses for the verb 'okulya' (to eat)

rows.english: he/she has eaten (and is now full)

tense_summary_table.important_note: The Everyday and Present Now forms look the same (alya). The time word or context tells you which tense is meant.

subject_prefixes.description: How the verb prefix changes depending on who is doing the action. These prefixes apply to present and everyday tenses. Past tense uses 'ya-' for he/she, 'na-' for I.

entries.subject: You plural (mwe)
entries.note: Flag cleared — Mugenda is correct standard Luganda for plural you.
